candidate_problem: Return-based evaluation conceals coordination failure modes in combinatorial MARL scaling
evidence_spans: Two policies may obtain similar return while relying on different interaction patterns, and conversely, a change in return may conflate poor coordination, inefficient division of labor, under-utilization of agents, or domain-specific bottlenecks.

These metrics reveal coordination failure modes that return alone can conceal (Figure 1), highlighting that in addition to measuring the return, cooperative MARL benchmarks should also assess how well agents coordinate with each other.
problem_evidence_spans: Most empirical evaluations of cooperative MARL rely primarily on outcome-level measures such as return, success rate, or completion time... These metrics are essential for measuring task performance, but they provide limited visibility into the coordination process
feasibility_evidence_spans: We release the STAT environment together with executable training and evaluation code for all methods considered in this paper... Code is available at https://github.com/mariacardei/coordination_aware_MARL .

scientific_hypothesis: In high-density task allocation (5A-100T), independent learning (IQL) will achieve returns within 5% of factorized methods (QMIX) but will exhibit a conflict rate at least 50% higher, demonstrating that return is an insufficient proxy for coordination efficiency.
expected_metric: Conflict Rate
success_threshold: Conflict rate for IQL > 1.5x QMIX while Return difference < 5%
failure_condition: IQL return is significantly lower (>15%) than QMIX, or Conflict Rates are statistically indistinguishable (p > 0.05).

baseline: Standard outcome-only evaluation (Episodic Return)
metric: Conflict Rate (total redundant assignments divided by total assignments)
story_angle: As we scale multi-agent systems, simply looking at the 'score' (reward) hides dangerous inefficiencies in how agents actually work together.

candidate_problem: Lack of statistical power in comparing model performance for high-dimensional genomic classification due to small cross-validation sample sizes.
evidence_spans: pairwise Wilcoxon signed-rank tests did not reach statistical significance at the 0.05 threshold (LR vs RF: p = 0.0625, LR vs SVM: p = 0.0625)

problem_evidence_spans: The observed performance differences should be interpreted as practically meaningful trends rather than formally validated conclusions.
feasibility_evidence_spans: Future work using repeated cross-validation or larger datasets may provide sufficient power for formal significance testing.

expected_metric: p-value from Wilcoxon signed-rank test on Macro F1 scores
success_threshold: p < 0.05 for the comparison between Logistic Regression and Random Forest.
failure_condition: p >= 0.05 despite increased sample size, suggesting the models are functionally equivalent in this domain.
first_24h_experiment: Download the TCGA-BRCA pre-processed gene expression matrix and run a 10x10 repeated cross-validation for Logistic Regression and Random Forest on the top 500 high-variance genes.

baseline: The paper's 5-fold cross-validation results (p = 0.0625).
story_angle: In the race for complex AI, we often ignore the statistical power required to prove they actually work better than simple regression.

baseline: The original 8.3 p.p. bias reported in the Agent Island paper.
metric: Percentage point (p.p.) increase in voting probability for same-provider finalists.
story_angle: As AI agents begin to govern each other, we've discovered they exhibit 'corporate tribalism'—favoring their own creators' models. Can we make them impartial?

candidate_problem: Inability of standard monotone response functions (e.g., Hill functions) to parsimoniously model non-monotone biological dynamics like overshoot and adaptation.
evidence_spans: The EML-generated module does not attempt to replace them when they work. Its purpose is to provide an alternative when the observed response is nonmonotone, adaptive, or shaped by competing positive and negative processes
The quantitative comparison in Sec. II.3 (AIC/BIC, Table S3) shows that the depth-1 EML gate G(R) beats the depth-1 Hill block H(R) by very large margins in all four panels
problem_evidence_spans: Standard saturating primitives, e.g. the Hill function, are monotone and require at least a difference of two such blocks to produce the same shape.
feasibility_evidence_spans: First, we apply an EML-ODE grammar to the PKA regulatory-subunit relocalization data of LaCroix et al. [ 13 ]

available_data_or_benchmark: Rho-GTPase recruitment data from Nanda et al. (2023) and PKA relocalization data from LaCroix et al. (2022).
scientific_hypothesis: A single EML-generated module (depth-1) can fit non-monotone biological time-series with significantly lower AIC/BIC than a single Hill function and comparable error to a sum of two Hill functions while using fewer parameters.
expected_metric: weighted Mean Squared Error (wMSE) on held-out time points.
success_threshold: EML depth-1 wMSE < 0.5 and BIC lower than Hill depth-1 across all four Nanda panels.
failure_condition: EML requires depth > 2 to match the accuracy of a single Hill function on monotone segments, or fails to converge on noisy time-series.
first_24h_experiment: Implement the EML operator eml(x,y) = exp(x) - ln(y) in a Python-based ODE solver (e.g., scipy.integrate.solve_ivp) and fit the parameters (a, b, c) to one trace of the Nanda et al. dataset using least squares.
specific_intervention: Replace the standard Hill-type activation term in a first-order relaxation ODE with the EML-generated module M1(R) = R^a - bR.
baseline: Hill-type ODE: dy/dt = (1/tau)*(-y + A*(R^h / (Kd^h + R^h))).
story_angle: The search for the 'atoms' of biological logic: how a single mathematical operator can replace complex mechanistic models for predicting cell signaling responses.

candidate_problem: Over-parameterization in Direct Coupling Analysis (DCA) models leads to restricted sequence space exploration and potential overfitting to noise in protein MSAs.
evidence_spans: Standard DCA approaches typically infer fully connected interaction graphs (bmDCA), resulting in dense models where the number of parameters often exceeds the information content of the training set.
This over-parameterization raises critical questions regarding overfitting and the true topology of the inferred landscape.
Does the inclusion of all possible couplings capture essential biological constraints, or does it introduce noise that artificially constrains the model?
problem_evidence_spans: Standard DCA approaches typically infer fully connected interaction graphs (bmDCA), resulting in dense models where the number of parameters often exceeds the information content of the training set.
feasibility_evidence_spans: We focus on the AroQ family of Chorismate Mutase (CM) enzymes [ 25 , 26 ] , a system previously established as a benchmark for evolution-based design [ 2 ] .
We validate these models experimentally using an in vivo complementation assay, testing their ability to generate functional enzymes across varying degrees of sequence divergence.
auto_research_experiment: Train a bmDCA model on the Chorismate Mutase MSA. Implement an iterative pruning (edDCA) protocol. Calculate the Shannon entropy at each sparsity level. Identify the meDCA (maximum entropy) point. Sample 1000 sequences from both the fully connected bmDCA and the meDCA. Compare the sequence diversity (Hamming distance) and the log-likelihood scores on a held-out set of natural CM sequences.
available_data_or_benchmark: AroQ family Chorismate Mutase (CM) Multiple Sequence Alignment (MSA).
public_training_or_eval_data: The paper references the CM system from Russ et al. (2020) Science, which provides the MSA and the E. coli complementation assay protocol.
scientific_hypothesis: Maximizing Shannon entropy during the sparsification of a Potts model for protein sequences identifies a regime that captures essential coevolutionary constraints while maximizing the volume of the functional sequence space compared to dense models.
expected_metric: Sequence Space Volume (effective number of sequences) and Log-likelihood on unseen natural sequences.
success_threshold: The meDCA model should assign higher log-likelihoods to unseen natural sequences and exhibit a sequence space volume at least 10 orders of magnitude larger than the fully connected bmDCA.
failure_condition: The meDCA model shows lower or equal log-likelihood on unseen sequences compared to the dense bmDCA, or the sequence diversity of generated functional sequences does not significantly increase.
first_24h_experiment: Download the CM MSA from public repositories. Train a standard bmDCA model using a package like ArDCA or PlmDCA. Implement a simple magnitude-based pruning of the J_ij coupling matrix and plot the Shannon entropy vs. sparsity curve.
specific_intervention: Iterative edge decimation (pruning) of the DCA coupling parameters (J_ij) to find the maximum entropy point (meDCA).
baseline: Fully connected Boltzmann Machine Direct Coupling Analysis (bmDCA).
metric: Shannon Entropy (H) and Log-likelihood (LL) of unseen natural sequences.
story_angle: By pruning the 'noise' out of protein models, we can discover billions of new functional protein sequences that nature hasn't explored yet.

scientific_hypothesis: The performance advantage of compartmentalized scVI is primarily due to biological signal localization rather than increased feature dimensionality.

success_threshold: Compartment-scVI (20-dim total) significantly outperforms Global-scVI (60-dim).

baseline: Global scVI (20-dim) AUROC of 0.725.
story_angle: Is bigger always better? Disentangling feature count from biological structure in single-cell disease models.

candidate_problem: Inaccurate viral protein-ligand binding affinity predictions due to un-split polyprotein sequences in training data
evidence_spans: Importantly, we found that 31% of viral protein binding data in BindingDb required polyprotein sequences to be carefully split before the data were suitable for training or testing ML models.
Fine-tuning DrugFormDTA on our custom cleaned antiviral dataset boosted performance from r =0.5 to r =0.7.
problem_evidence_spans: 31% of viral protein binding data in BindingDb required polyprotein sequences to be carefully split before the data were suitable for training or testing ML models.
feasibility_evidence_spans: We then presented our custom dataset of 43,005 viral protein-ligand binding measurements that we curated from BindingDb and other sources.
Fine-tuning DrugFormDTA on our custom cleaned antiviral dataset boosted performance from r =0.5 to r =0.7.
auto_research_experiment: Train three state-of-the-art DTA models (e.g., Boltz-2, GNINA, and Interformer) on two versions of the BindingDb viral subset: one with raw polyprotein sequences and one with the paper's 43,005 split-sequence dataset. Compare the Pearson correlation (r) and RMSE across 16 specific viral protein targets.
available_data_or_benchmark: The paper's custom dataset of 43,005 viral protein-ligand binding measurements and the test set of 853 antiviral compounds.
scientific_hypothesis: Explicitly splitting viral polyproteins into functional domains for DTA model training significantly reduces sequence-length-induced noise and improves the Pearson correlation of binding affinity predictions by at least 0.15 across diverse viral families.
expected_metric: Pearson correlation coefficient (r)
success_threshold: r > 0.65 for the split-sequence model vs r < 0.55 for the raw-sequence model
failure_condition: The improvement in r is less than 0.05 or the model fails to generalize to the 16 protein targets in the test set.
first_24h_experiment: Download the 43,005 curated measurements from the Radvac dashboard, extract the DrugFormDTA architecture, and run a 5-fold cross-validation comparing raw vs. split sequences on a single target (e.g., SARS-CoV-2 Mpro).
specific_intervention: Fine-tune the DrugFormDTA model using the polyprotein-split sequence dataset vs. the original BindingDb unsplit sequences.
baseline: DrugFormDTA trained on standard unsplit BindingDb viral data (r=0.50).
story_angle: The 'Polyprotein Trap': Why current AI drug discovery models are failing on viruses by ignoring how viral proteins actually mature.

candidate_problem: Instability and lack of reproducibility in CFN dependency matrices
evidence_spans: The global CFN edge Jaccard of 0.026 indicates near-zero cross-fold reproducibility
the specific dependency structures responsible for the performance advantage cannot be identified from the current data.

scientific_hypothesis: Increasing donor sample size to N > 100 will stabilize the CFN dependency matrix, yielding a cross-fold Jaccard index > 0.5 for top edges.
expected_metric: Edge Jaccard Index
success_threshold: Mean cross-fold Jaccard > 0.4 for top 20 edges.
failure_condition: Jaccard remains < 0.1 despite increased sample size, suggesting CFN is inherently unstable for compositional data.
first_24h_experiment: Harmonize cell type labels between SCP259 and a second public IBD dataset to create a larger donor pool.
specific_intervention: Increase donor sample size and apply L1 regularization to the CFN dependency matrix.
baseline: Global CFN Jaccard of 0.026.
story_angle: When 'interpretable' models lie: fixing the instability of dependency networks in small-sample clinical genomics.

candidate_problem: High-dimensional redundancy in SSL speech features causes diffusion manifold drift and catastrophic failure in zero-shot TTS.
evidence_spans: Directly feeding the 1024-dim WavLM-Large features into a diffusion transformer produces catastrophic failure—a 338M-parameter DiT trained under this setting yields an unreadable WER of 110% on zero-shot TTS
The massive redundancy of SSL feature spaces exacerbates manifold drift in diffusion models, yielding off-manifold latents that degrade decoding fidelity.
problem_evidence_spans: Directly feeding the 1024-dim WavLM-Large features into a diffusion transformer produces catastrophic failure—a 338M-parameter DiT trained under this setting yields an unreadable WER of 110% on zero-shot TTS
feasibility_evidence_spans: Stage 1 utilizes a symmetric adapter-based auto-encoder to distill frozen WavLM-Large features into a 128-dim bottleneck
WavCube approaches SSL upperbound on SUPERB despite 8 × 8\times dimensional compression
auto_research_experiment: Train a Diffusion Transformer (DiT) on the LibriSpeech dataset using three different latent representations: 1) Raw 1024-dim WavLM features, 2) 128-dim PCA-reduced WavLM features, and 3) 128-dim Auto-Encoder (AE) distilled features (WavCube Stage 1). Compare the Word Error Rate (WER) and Speaker Similarity (SIM-o) after 50k training steps.
available_data_or_benchmark: LibriSpeech (for TTS training/eval), SUPERB (for understanding eval).
scientific_hypothesis: A non-linear information bottleneck (Auto-Encoder) is superior to linear dimensionality reduction (PCA) or raw high-dimensional features for diffusion-based speech generation because it carves a compact, diffusion-friendly subspace while preserving semantic topology.
expected_metric: Word Error Rate (WER)
success_threshold: WER < 5.0% on LibriSpeech test-clean using the 128-dim AE latent.
failure_condition: WER > 50% or SIM-o < 0.3, indicating the generator cannot recover intelligible speech from the compressed latent.
first_24h_experiment: Extract 1024-dim WavLM-Large features for the LibriSpeech train-960 set and train a simple 3-layer MLP Auto-Encoder to compress them to 128-dim. Measure reconstruction loss (MSE) and visualize the latent space using t-SNE against ESC-50 classes.
specific_intervention: Apply a symmetric adapter-based auto-encoder bottleneck to reduce WavLM-Large features from 1024 to 128 dimensions before feeding into a Diffusion Transformer.
baseline: Raw 1024-dim WavLM-Large features fed directly into the same DiT architecture.
story_angle: The 'curse of dimensionality' in speech foundation models: why more data in the latent space actually breaks generative AI, and how a simple bottleneck fixes it.

baseline: Llama-3.3-70B-Inst performance on SEQUOR Single regime (30.00% drop).

candidate_problem: Standard Adam optimizer's momentum term allows for deterministic movement in the absence of selection pressure, which is evolutionarily impossible and violates biological fidelity in digital evolution simulations.
evidence_spans: A notable outlier, however, is the state-of-the-art Adam optimizer. In order to be brought into strict evolutionary compliance, Adam’s momentum term requires some minor but principled mathematical surgery.

A notable outlier is Adam, whose inertial momentum term permits deterministic movement in the absence of selection pressure — an evolutionary impossibility. The principled repair performed in Sec. 3.2 replaces its additive momentum with a rank-1 projection that amplifies selection pressure along historically successful directions.
problem_evidence_spans: A notable outlier is Adam, whose inertial momentum term permits deterministic movement in the absence of selection pressure — an evolutionary impossibility.
feasibility_evidence_spans: Code available at https://github.com/danielgrimmer/adam-dls
As the Rosenbrock benchmark (Fig. 5 ) confirms, this surgery preserves Adam’s capacity to navigate ill-conditioned landscapes.
auto_research_experiment: Implement the 'mathematical surgery' on Adam (replacing additive momentum with the rank-1 projection described in Sec 3.2) and compare its performance and evolutionary fidelity against standard Adam and SGD on the Rosenbrock function and a Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) loss landscape.
available_data_or_benchmark: Rosenbrock function; Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) loss landscapes.
scientific_hypothesis: Replacing Adam's additive momentum with a rank-1 projection (DLS-Adam) maintains the optimization efficiency of Adam on ill-conditioned landscapes while ensuring that parameter updates are strictly driven by selection pressure (gradient-dependent), satisfying evolutionary first principles.
expected_metric: Convergence speed and final loss value.
success_threshold: DLS-Adam achieves a final loss within 5% of standard Adam on the Rosenbrock benchmark while maintaining zero update magnitude when the gradient is zero.
failure_condition: DLS-Adam fails to converge on the Rosenbrock function or requires >2x the iterations of standard Adam to reach the same loss threshold.
first_24h_experiment: Clone the provided repository, run the existing Rosenbrock benchmark (Fig 5) comparing SGD, Adam, and the modified DLS-Adam, and verify that DLS-Adam's update vector becomes null when the gradient is manually zeroed.
specific_intervention: Replace the standard Adam momentum update (m_t = beta*m_{t-1} + (1-beta)*g_t) with the rank-1 projection 'surgery' described in Section 3.2 of the paper.
baseline: Standard Adam optimizer (Kingma & Ba, 2014).
story_angle: Bridging the gap between high-performance AI optimizers and biological reality by fixing the 'evolutionary impossibility' of Adam's momentum.

candidate_problem: Performance gap between one-stage co-design and two-stage (ProteinMPNN-based) protein design models
evidence_spans: Most crucially, although the performance gap between the two-stage (with ProteinMPNN) and one-stage co-design models has been shrunk with our new state-of-the-art results, it remains non-negligible.
problem_evidence_spans: it remains non-negligible
feasibility_evidence_spans: A-CODE achieves strong empirical performance and provides a flexible, unified foundation
PXDesign [ 26 ] extends Protenix [ 10 ] , an open-source reimplementation of AF3
auto_research_experiment: Implement a hybrid training objective for A-CODE that incorporates a ProteinMPNN-style cross-entropy loss on the inferred residue types during the denoising steps to enforce stronger sequence-structure compatibility.
available_data_or_benchmark: PDB (Protein Data Bank) and the unconditional protein generation benchmark used in the paper.
scientific_hypothesis: Integrating an auxiliary inverse-folding loss during the joint diffusion process will bridge the designability gap between one-stage and two-stage models.
expected_metric: scTM (self-consistency Template Modeling score) and scRMSD.
success_threshold: A-CODE-hybrid achieves scTM > 0.5 on at least 80% of generated samples, matching or exceeding ProteinMPNN-based cascades.
failure_condition: The auxiliary loss causes the diffusion process to diverge or results in lower structural diversity (mode collapse).
first_24h_experiment: Fine-tune the pre-trained A-CODE model on a subset of PDB for 1000 steps with an added residue-level cross-entropy loss and evaluate scTM on 100 samples.
specific_intervention: Add a residue-level cross-entropy loss term to the A-CODE training objective, calculated by mapping predicted atom types back to residue identities.
baseline: Original A-CODE (one-stage) and PXDesign + ProteinMPNN (two-stage).
story_angle: Closing the gap between end-to-end protein generation and modular cascades.

candidate_problem: High variance and target-specific performance drops in ML-based binding affinity prediction for diverse viral species
evidence_spans: We found that Boltz-2 and DrugFormDTA ranked highest overall among ML-based approaches, and GNINA did best among docking approaches, with notable variance across specific viral proteins.
problem_evidence_spans: notable variance across specific viral proteins.
feasibility_evidence_spans: We then benchmarked 15 open-source binding affinity prediction tools on a custom test set of 853 antiviral compounds spread across 16 different protein targets from 10 virus species.
auto_research_experiment: Perform a systematic 'leave-one-virus-family-out' cross-validation using the 15 benchmarked tools. Identify which structural features of viral proteins (using the 16 targets) correlate with the highest prediction error in Boltz-2 and GNINA.
available_data_or_benchmark: The Radvac test set of 853 compounds across 16 targets and 10 virus species.
scientific_hypothesis: ML-based DTA models (Boltz-2) outperform physics-based docking (GNINA) on viral proteases but underperform on viral polymerases due to the lack of diverse polymerase-ligand co-crystal structures in training sets.
expected_metric: Mean Absolute Error (MAE) per protein class
success_threshold: Identification of a specific protein class where MAE is >2x the global mean, followed by a 20% error reduction via targeted data augmentation.
failure_condition: Variance is found to be random or purely driven by data quantity rather than protein structural class.
first_24h_experiment: Categorize the 16 targets into functional classes (Protease, Polymerase, etc.) and calculate the per-class RMSE for the top 3 models (Boltz-2, DrugFormDTA, GNINA) using the paper's reported results.
specific_intervention: Class-specific fine-tuning of DrugFormDTA using the 43,005 measurement dataset, stratified by viral protein function.
baseline: Global DrugFormDTA performance (r=0.70).
story_angle: Mapping the 'Blind Spots' of AI in the viral protein landscape to ensure we don't miss the next broad-spectrum antiviral.

candidate_problem: Lack of standardized benchmarks and datasets for non-canonical amino acid (ncAA) generation
evidence_spans: Furthermore, our evaluations of the ncAA generations remain preliminary, as no widely acknowledged datasets or benchmarks have been established.
problem_evidence_spans: no widely acknowledged datasets or benchmarks have been established
feasibility_evidence_spans: A-CODE demonstrates the ability to generate structurally plausible ncAA
our framework naturally extends to ncAA modeling, which existing models fail due to their hard-coded constraints
auto_research_experiment: Curate a dataset of ncAA-containing proteins from the PDB and develop a 'ncAA-Recovery' benchmark where the model must reconstruct ncAA identities and side-chain geometries from masked structures.
available_data_or_benchmark: PDB (Protein Data Bank) filtered for non-standard residues (HETATM records).
scientific_hypothesis: A-CODE's atom-centric diffusion can recover ncAA identities and conformations with higher accuracy than traditional residue-based models adapted for ncAAs.
expected_metric: ncAA-RMSD and Type-Accuracy.
success_threshold: Type-Accuracy > 70% for the top 50 most frequent ncAAs in PDB.
failure_condition: The model consistently predicts standard amino acids (e.g., Serine) instead of chemically similar ncAAs.
first_24h_experiment: Extract all instances of Selenocysteine and Phosphoserine from PDB, mask them, and run A-CODE inference to check recovery rate.
specific_intervention: Create a standardized evaluation pipeline that calculates the RMSD of predicted ncAA atoms against ground truth PDB structures.
baseline: A-CODE (zero-shot) vs. Rosetta-based ncAA placement.
story_angle: Establishing the first rigorous benchmark for the 'wild west' of non-canonical protein design.
